topic: Someone finds a time capsule they don't remember burying
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the dusty outline of the metal box with a fingertip, the cool metal a stark contrast to the simmering heat in his chest. It had been unearthed from the back of his overgrown garden, a forgotten relic of a past he couldn't quite grasp. He hadn't put it there. He truly hadn't. The sheer absurdity of the find made a tight knot form beneath his ribs. He lifted the lid, the scent of aged paper and dry lavender wafting out, and began to sift through the contents. Each faded photograph, each brittle letter, felt like an accusation—a reminder of commitments he’d abandoned, of promises he’d let slip through his fingers. He dropped a brittle, yellowed ticket stub, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet afternoon. He just wanted it gone, buried again, somewhere deep and undisturbed, far from this unwelcome excavation of memory.

[story 2]

“What is this junk?” Sarah muttered, kicking at the heavy, sealed container half-buried beneath the loose soil near the old oak. Her shoulders slumped, the effort of digging feeling like a pointless chore. She hadn't intended to look for anything specific, just clearing the weeds. But when she pried the lid open, the contents—a collection of childish drawings and stiff, formal declarations—hit her with a dull thud of unwelcome recognition. A tightness constricted her throat. She looked at the dates etched on the cardboard backing, and a wave of profound dissatisfaction washed over her. Why was she even bothering to look for things now? Everything felt like a burden, and this box was just another weight she hadn't asked for. She shoved the box back into the earth with unnecessary force, the dirt clinging to her palms.

[story 3]

I stared at the time capsule, the heavy lid mocking me from the damp earth. It was sealed tight, a perfect little coffin for some forgotten moment. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I remember nothing of burying it; it was just *there*, unearthed by some clumsy excavation. Every item inside—the faded newspaper clippings, the childish scribbles—felt like evidence of choices I’d made that left gaping holes in my present. I ran a hand over my tired face, the skin feeling tight and unfamiliar. A low, bitter sound escaped my lips. This whole discovery was just another thing to sort through, another layer of history I wasn't meant to own. I wanted the ground to swallow it whole, to erase the evidence of what used to be.
